coaching.coaching: Pin down lang key type and normalization contract with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: lang key type and normalization contract

coaching.coaching: Pin down accruing_since concrete value and type with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: accruing_since concrete value and type

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for analyzer protocol and resolver architectural decision — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: analyzer protocol and resolver architectural decision

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `lang` key type and normalization contract in AC 2 or the Description (e.g., 'lowercase string, e.g. `"python"`; `resolve_analyzer` normalizes via `.lower().strip()` before lookup') so all downstream tickets use a consistent key.
evidence: AC 2 reads: '`resolve_analyzer(lang)` returns None when none configured' — `lang` type and normalization are not specified
Downstream tickets 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0 will implement adapters that register under a lang key; 0734 will call resolve_analyzer — key contract must be consistent across all of them
Existing codebase has no lang/language concept to inherit a convention from; this is a genuinely novel interface with no prior art in src/rebar/metrics/
findings.finding: The `lang` key type and normalization contract for `resolve_analyzer(lang)` is unspecified in the plan. Since all 6 downstream tickets (9597, e7a0, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, 0734) must register and look up analyzers by this key, a mismatch (e.g., 'Python' vs 'python') silently returns None and renders every code_health metric Unavailable in production — a hidden ordering/atomicity assumption baked into the interface.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria – AC 2: `resolve_analyzer(lang)` signature; Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py
scenarios: Adapter ticket registers 'Python' (capital P); metric reader calls resolve_analyzer('python') -> silently returns None -> every code_health metric becomes Unavailable with no error
Two adapter tickets independently pick different casing/naming ('py' vs 'python') -> ANALYZERS dict has multiple keys, neither matches the caller
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 2: '`lang` is a lowercase string (e.g. `"python"`); `resolve_analyzer` normalizes the input with `.lower().strip()` before dict lookup so adapter registration and caller spelling can't silently diverge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (or a test case) to the Verification section that asserts `import rebar.metrics.analyzer` does not mutate `REGISTRY` (e.g. capture `len(REGISTRY)` before and after import and assert equality).
evidence: AC3: 'Pure stdlib; import registers nothing into REGISTRY as a side effect (opt-in wiring lands in 0734)'
Verification section only names: 'Unit: miss -> Unavailable(reason, accruing_since); support -> AnalyzerResult passthrough. `make lint` + `pytest tests/unit/metrics`'
ABSENCE: no test case described for the no-side-effect import invariant
findings.finding: The AC 'Pure stdlib; import registers nothing into REGISTRY as a side effect' has no corresponding test or proving command in the Verification section, so it can be silently violated.
scenarios: A future refactor accidentally adds a module-level registration call; no test catches it because the invariant was never asserted.
An executor adds an `__init__.py` that imports and registers something; CI stays green because the proving command never checks REGISTRY state.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Verification section: `python -c "from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY; before=set(REGISTRY); import rebar.metrics.analyzer; assert set(REGISTRY)==before, 'side-effect detected'"` or an equivalent pytest fixture that snapshots and compares REGISTRY before/after import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete source of `accruing_since` for the fallback `Unavailable` (e.g., 'pass the ticket's `accruing_since` constant, or accept it as a parameter to the fallback helper, to keep the value stable and semantically correct').
evidence: AC 2: 'fallback helper returns the reused `Unavailable(reason="no analyzer configured for <lang>", accruing_since=<ts>)` — `<ts>` is a placeholder with no specified source
registry.py:89: `accruing_since: str` — typed as ISO-8601 string
registry.py:140,148,156: all existing usages pass stable hardcoded ISO-8601 timestamps, never a dynamic value
The CLI renders `accruing_since` directly into JSON output (metrics.py:75); a dynamic value would produce non-deterministic output across calls
findings.finding: The `accruing_since` value for the fallback `Unavailable` is written as a placeholder (`<ts>`) with no specification of its source. The existing `Unavailable` dataclass types this field as `str` (ISO-8601), and every existing usage passes a stable, hardcoded timestamp (e.g. `'2026-07-18T00:00:00+00:00'`). A dynamically-generated `datetime.now()` would make the fallback's `accruing_since` non-deterministic per-call, breaking test assertions and the CLI's serialized output contract.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections: 'fallback helper returns `Unavailable(reason=..., accruing_since=<ts>)`'; Acceptance criteria AC 2
scenarios: Implementer uses `datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc).isoformat()` -> each call returns a different timestamp -> test assertions comparing Unavailable objects fail or become flaky
Implementer hardcodes an arbitrary epoch date -> `accruing_since` misrepresents when the code_health signal actually began accruing
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `<ts>` with a concrete instruction: define a module-level `_ACCRUING_SINCE` constant (mirroring `git_metrics.py` and `event_metrics.py` patterns) and pass it as `accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE` in the fallback helper, or accept `accruing_since` as a parameter to the fallback function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete `accruing_since` default in the plan (e.g. `datetime.now(timezone.utc)`) so the executor uses the same clock source as the rest of the codebase.
evidence: 'accruing_since=<ts>' — `<ts>` is a placeholder, not a stated default
The plan does not name the clock source (UTC, local, monotonic) or the stdlib call to use
findings.finding: The `accruing_since=<ts>` argument in the fallback helper is written as a placeholder (`<ts>`) without specifying the concrete value to use (e.g. `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` or `datetime.utcnow()`), leaving the executor to choose the timestamp source.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections / Acceptance criteria AC2: 'Unavailable(reason="no analyzer configured for <lang>", accruing_since=<ts>)'
scenarios: An executor uses `datetime.utcnow()` (deprecated in 3.12) while the rest of the codebase uses `datetime.now(timezone.utc)`, introducing a subtle inconsistency in sentinel values.
An executor uses `time.time()` (float epoch) while callers expect a `datetime` object, causing downstream rendering failures.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC2 description, replace `accruing_since=<ts>` with `accruing_since=datetime.now(timezone.utc)` (matching the existing Unavailable usage pattern in metrics/registry.py).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md entry capturing: (1) why `Protocol` over ABC, (2) why `ANALYZERS` map + `resolve_analyzer` over a class-based registry, (3) the `analyzers/` package boundary rule, and (4) the `Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)` sentinel reuse rationale — so the 6 blocked tickets have a durable design reference.
evidence: 'Foundation seam for the code_health lens: a language-agnostic analyzer protocol'
'Dependencies: None (keystone; blocks 9597, e7a0, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, 0734)'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc in Files likely touched or Verification sections
Plan records the WHAT but not the WHY in any durable artifact
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural keystone (analyzer Protocol + resolver pattern) that explicitly blocks 6 downstream tickets, but includes no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc capture of the decision — leaving the rationale (why Protocol vs. ABC, why `ANALYZERS` map vs. a class-based registry, why `analyzers/` package vs. other locations, why reuse `Unavailable` sentinel) undocumented for future maintainers and the 6 dependent teams.
findings.location: Description / Dependencies section ('blocks 9597, e7a0, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, 0734') / Files likely touched
scenarios: A developer working on ticket e7a0 or 3ba0 (later adapters) questions why Protocol was chosen over ABC or why the package lives in `analyzers/` vs. `adapters/` and finds no durable rationale, leading to inconsistent pattern application.
The `Unavailable` reuse rationale (sentinel shape matching CLI rendering) is only in the ticket description; once the ticket is closed it becomes opaque to new contributors.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-code-health-analyzer-interface.md` (or add a section to AGENTS.md) covering: chosen pattern (Protocol for structural subtyping, no forced inheritance), resolver design (ANALYZERS dict, opt-in wiring deferred to 0734), package location rule (`src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` — not `adapters/`), and sentinel reuse (`Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)` from `rebar.metrics.registry`). Reference it in the ticket's Files likely touched list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile 'null/default analyzer' in the Description with AC: either add an AC that explicitly names and verifies the artifact (e.g. a `NullAnalyzer` class) or rewrite the Description to say the 'null fallback' is the None-return + Unavailable sentinel pattern rather than a concrete class.
evidence: Description: 'ship the protocol, the selector, and a null/default analyzer so the contract is testable in isolation'
AC: 'resolve_analyzer(lang) returns None when none configured; fallback helper returns the reused Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
No AC item verifies the existence or behavior of a class/object called 'null/default analyzer'
findings.finding: The description names 'a null/default analyzer' as a distinct deliverable, but no AC explicitly covers creating or verifying this artifact. The AC instead specifies `resolve_analyzer(lang)` returning None + a 'fallback helper' returning Unavailable — which may or may not constitute a class named NullAnalyzer. An implementer reading the description would expect a concrete NullAnalyzer class; the AC would accept mere None-return. This mismatch risks either an untested artifact or a missing one.
findings.location: Description ('a null/default analyzer') vs AC line 1 ('resolve_analyzer(lang) returns None when none configured')
scenarios: Implementer ships a NullAnalyzer class that is never verified by any AC.
Implementer ships only None+Unavailable and the description's 'null/default analyzer' is a dead concept causing confusion in code review.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a null/default analyzer' in the Description with 'a resolve_analyzer(lang) selector that returns None when no analyzer is configured, plus a fallback helper returning Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)' to match the AC exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the fallback helper explicitly in the plan (e.g. `make_unavailable(lang: str) -> Unavailable`) and specify it as a module-level function in `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`, so the Verification step can reference it directly.
evidence: AC: 'fallback helper returns the reused Unavailable(reason, accruing_since) — NEVER scans a path, fabricates zeros, or leaks an exception string'
Review-driven corrections: 'the fallback helper returns Unavailable(reason=..., accruing_since=<ts>)'
Files likely touched: only `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py` listed — no indication of where fallback helper lives or what it's called
findings.finding: The 'fallback helper' is referenced in both Review-driven corrections and AC but is never given a name, signature, or file location anywhere in the plan. Implementers don't know whether it is a standalone function, a method on a class, or inline code at the call site.
findings.location: AC line 2 ('fallback helper returns the reused Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)') and Review-driven corrections ('the fallback helper returns Unavailable(...)')
scenarios: One implementer puts fallback logic inline at call sites; another creates a module-level function `unavailable_for_lang(lang)`. Neither violates the AC as written, causing inconsistency.
Verification step cannot directly reference the helper because it has no name.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'A module-level `make_unavailable(lang: str) -> Unavailable` function in `analyzer.py` is the canonical fallback helper; Verification must import and call it directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` (stub package) is created in this ticket or deferred to the first adapter ticket; add it to 'Files likely touched' if this ticket owns it.
evidence: Plan states: 'New adapters (later tickets) live in a NEW `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` package'
`src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` does not exist in the repo (list_directory confirmed)
Files likely touched only lists `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py` (singular) and `tests/unit/metrics/test_analyzer.py` — no mention of the `analyzers/` package stub
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` (plural) as the future package for adapters, but does NOT list even a stub `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` as a file touched in this ticket; if the package skeleton is meant to be established here as part of the seam, it is an unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: Files likely touched / Review-driven corrections ('New adapters … live in a NEW `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` package')
scenarios: A downstream ticket (e.g. 9597) attempts to place an adapter in `rebar.metrics.analyzers` but the package `__init__.py` was never created, causing an ImportError.
A reviewer implementing this ticket creates `analyzer.py` (singular) but is unsure whether to also scaffold the `analyzers/` package or leave it for later.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to the Description for why `Protocol` structural subtyping is chosen (e.g., 'Protocol is used so adapter modules in `analyzers/` need not import from `analyzer.py`, keeping the dependency graph acyclic and adapters independently testable').
evidence: Description says 'ship the protocol, the selector, and a null/default analyzer so the contract is testable in isolation' — states WHAT but not WHY Protocol over alternatives
No rationale for why structural subtyping (Protocol) is preferred (e.g., 'adapters need not import this module; duck-typed for isolation') vs ABC (explicit inheritance) or callable protocol
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing Protocol structural subtyping over alternatives. While Protocol is the correct choice here, its absence means an implementer has no guidance on *why* the interface is designed this way — particularly relevant since this is a keystone blocking 6 tickets and Protocol vs ABC vs plain callable have meaningfully different implications for how downstream adapters are authored.
scenarios: Implementer second-guesses the Protocol choice and uses ABC inheritance, making downstream adapter tickets import from analyzer.py and creating the circular import risk the plan explicitly avoids

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `Runner` Protocol in `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` as prior art and explicitly state whether `Analyzer` should mirror its `@runtime_checkable` decorator and `FakeAnalyzer`/`NullAnalyzer` null-object naming convention.
evidence: Plan AC: '`Analyzer` protocol + `AnalyzerResult`' with no mention of @runtime_checkable or naming convention for null-object
Existing prior art: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` lines 85-99 defines `@runtime_checkable class Runner(Protocol)` with a `FakeRunner` null-object — the direct in-repo template for exactly this pattern
Plan description names 'a null/default analyzer' but does not call it FakeAnalyzer, NullAnalyzer, or any specific name — inconsistency risk vs established FakeRunner idiom
ABSENCE: no cross-reference to Runner Protocol in plan text
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `Analyzer` Protocol + null-object pattern but does not acknowledge the directly analogous `Runner` Protocol (in `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`) as prior art, leaving unresolved whether the new protocol should mirror Runner's `@runtime_checkable` decorator and FakeRunner null-object conventions already established in this repo.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Description / Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py
scenarios: Implementer omits @runtime_checkable, breaking isinstance() checks that downstream consumers (tickets 9597, e7a0, 18f9) may rely on — inconsistent with how Runner is tested via isinstance(runner, Runner)
Null-object is named differently from FakeRunner convention, creating friction when consumers mix Runner and Analyzer injection patterns
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prior art' note in the plan: 'Mirror the established `Runner` Protocol shape: decorate `Analyzer` with `@runtime_checkable` (enables `isinstance` checks in DI), and name the null-object `FakeAnalyzer` for consistency with `FakeRunner`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
